speaker: interviewer
text: 1995, And we are interviewing survivor Abraham Melezin.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I-- they showed me-- they showed me the two German. Somebody covered him up. And I went to the director of that-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I said, do you know that we all will be killed? He said, yes, we should go away. We should go away.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I ran upstairs where I lived. I grabbed some more money in the pocket, whatever I had there. Essentially, these are-- were German money, which I got to give to Krzanowski, he should make proper account with them.

speaker: interviewee
text: Came down, and I said, put the horse in the cart. And the moment finished up preparing the horse, here is two truckloads of SS, or German-- no, it was Wehrmacht. What is very interesting, the German soldier who was hit right into the temple. You know-- I would-- how could-- how they could hit him on the temple, I don't know, but-- so everybody out, everybody. They put everybody. Who is the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the German kid, whatever it is-- she also came out, right-- everybody.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened? So he said, I don't know. Probably Soviet partisans probably came in the evening. And it seems to me that the policeman spotted them. And we started to shoot. And-- and so the German who had the automatic gun, and this what happened. So everybody in a row, in a line. And he started to go around and around. He start with me. You, he said, he took me right behind the house, took six soldiers. And would-- and-- took from the-- take the handkerchief and put around his eyes.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, I-- I said, if-- so I said, you mean-- no, I forgot that I don't know German. I said in German, if I have to die, so I can die without it. No, he said, put him. So a soldier came up to me. And at that moment, came up Krzanowski, and said to the officer something-- whispered something in the ear. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I remember, [NON-ENGLISH]. Let him go. So, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] they didn't touch anybody. They went on the trucks and moved away.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I came up to Krzanowki, I told him, you [INAUDIBLE]-- what did you tell him that suddenly he change? He said, very simple. I said that you are married to a Jewish woman. And, obviously, if you are married to a Jewish woman, you wouldn't do it-- you wouldn't do anything. You are innocent. I said, do you know that if I write a Jewish woman, I am liable to be shot? This is the official thing? Yes, so run away. So, you know, so I run back, you know-- back. I took some more valuables. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I put certain things-- I had-- I bought a pair of German boots with-- German boots were that had on the soles nails. But in between here, I stuck in some golden pieces that were hidden in other pieces.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the other pieces, I stuck in all the documents, you know, and ran out. And I took-- look, look, I told him, I'm going to [NON-ENGLISH], that means, you know, where supposing my wife is. But-- oh, and you will get tomorrow, the horse you will get at [NON-ENGLISH] What I did, what I thought, if I leave somewhere, I tell him I'll go kilometer or two. I turn around the horse, and then run across the fields and some swamps, you know, to Rulewicze. And the entrance to Zapole was a beautiful alley from old timber, you know, everything green. The moment I reached the end, with the horse, the end of the alley, here it comes across-- another truck with four policemen and four German SS, took me off.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I said, I have to bring the horse back. So the German tells me, turn it around. So I turn it around. And the horse ran. They took me away on the truck, put me in jail. The next day-- this probably was September third or fourth. The next day, they put me-- whether I am Jewish. I said, no. But you have Jewish wife? I said, no, I said. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Yeah, there was interesting conversation before I left with Krzanowski. How do you know that I have a Jewish wife, I ask him. He said, look, about three or four weeks ago, [NON-ENGLISH] wanted PP, and he went to [NON-ENGLISH]. And she saw that he's-- said, I why didn't tell you that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I said, [NON-ENGLISH] kept quiet. But it seems that she didn't keep-- you know. Well, this is when, you know, what happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I said, look, if you want to go to inspect me, I want a doctor. All right, they took me to the hospital. There was a woman doctor, a white Russian. If there were poor, it would be different. So she inspected me. You are circumcised. Yes, I said, it is not a regular circumcision, not Jewish circumcision, I said. I come from Georgia, [NON-ENGLISH]. And I got there, you know, one tropical disease, phimosis. So my parents took me all the way to Warsaw. And I named the doctor who made the operation. It is what it is. And it don't make any difference. I was in Polish army as a Pole. And nobody ever thought that I am Jewish. So she said to me, all right, I will relay what you say. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They put me in a cell with somebody. In Kapole-- in a day or so, I was called in downstairs.

speaker: interviewee
text: They took away money, whatever I had, everything. And I told them, look, please, this belongs to the government. So I shouldn't-- you should remember it. The money will got to Kzarnowski, he will make an account. He knows what. My own money is only 500 rubles. They took me. And they gave me a white Russian interpreter. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So I told to the German officer, you know, who supposed to interrogate me, I said, look, I am a Pole. And I don't want to a white Russian. Give me a German, because I don't know how he will translate. I don't know-- I forgot that I spoke to the German officer in German there. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I don't know how he will translate [INAUDIBLE] properly. All right, he started interrogation.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you a Pole? No. Why are you circumcised? I told him the story. Where is your wife? I said, my wife-- you're asking me where is my wife? I am three days out of the estate. You tell me where is my wife.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're Jewish. You're Jewish from Warsaw, he says. I am not Jewish from Warsaw. The man who speaks German, he knows Polish, so he sees that my Polish pronunciation is Russian. Is it because I spent a lot of time in Russia with my parents when I was a child, and I was a typical Russian accent. All right, we said, we'll take a look. What he did, he told me to take off everything. Take a ruler, put my hand on the table, and started-- you know, to-- all the time, all the time with the ruler.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, it's 15-- I felt it that it gets numb. So I try to-- he said, no, keep it on. So he gave me a-- so I kept. And after few minutes, are you Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, no. He did the same, the second hand. And then he called in an SS man, a German. And both hands on the table, and they start on both hands. It lasted I don't know how long. I felt that everything gets numb. Then they put me on the ground floor, took off my boots, and two of them hit here and two hit me here. After that, I had these-- when I came to United States, I had some spurs. And the doctor thought this is because of being beaten.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right, I didn't say anything. I didn't say anything. They sent me home-- to my cell-- [LAUGHS] you know. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the next day, again. He burned my hand-- are you Jewish? No, I am not. What do you know about the [POLISH]? [POLISH] is Polish. You know, they called it Polish partisans. I said, I don't know anything. I have nothing to do with them. I said, I said, be logical. If you say that I'm Jewish, so how would a Jew will be in Polish partisans? It's-- no, so he does this and all, all the time.

speaker: interviewee
text: He looked at me, probably surprised by the argument. You are Jewish. All things, you are Jewish. Your child is [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] circumscribed. So I said, yes, you know, in fact, I say, I try to save-- I try to save for a Jewish woman and her child. Ah, you're Jewish. They bound me here with a rope, a long rope. I didn't know what they were going to do. And then put me between the doors. And one of the SS men pulled the rope, and I fell. And then when I fell, I saw that there is a hook. He put the rope over, and pulled me up, down. And the interrogator ask, all right, you will be here, you know, hang here, until you decide whether you want to tell the truth or not. And I was there, you know-- better there than hiding. And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] when I came back to the cell, I tried to massage that, you know. But still it was-- I couldn't do anything. So I hanged-- all right, I hanged the higher-- there was something, quite probably blood started to flow from my nose or from ears. I don't know. I saw drops of blood. And they came in. And he abruptly released the rope. But I suspected that they'll do it so I hit my head. So I succeeded to fall on my back. Next day-- next day, again. But this time they called in-- he called in SS men. Sent away the interpreter-- the German interpreter. They put me on the floor. They took a whip with lead on the end, and started to lash me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what I tried just to keep myself tense muscles to permit. But I was a very well built, you know, muscular man at the time. He beat me. He beat me. He beat me. He beat me. Are you? No, I am not. He sent me away. Next day again, he beat me. He beat me, I don't know.

speaker: interviewee
text: Around the 15th-- I count the days, you know-- around the 15th, there were so many times. And then what has happened, you know, one day he beat me. And I fainted. But somewhere, I felt that he stopped beating me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And all the time, I repeated, you don't remember names? You don't want anybody? You never find it. You never--

speaker: interviewee
text: You are [NON-ENGLISH], you know only Polish and Russian. I knocked my head all the time I tried to hypnotize myself, so to speak. I don't know how to. The next day, he start to beat me again, you know, to-- to fling me. So after two, I sort of-- but not because I did. I did myself. I faked. He stopped beating. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] They threw water on me, and took me to the cell. I came to back at the cell. I repeated-- I repeated this same-- two or three, I don't remember, several times, let's say. Then according to my count, around 15th of September, there was a big commotion from some cells. People are taken out. People are taken out. In my cell, during these days, at night, they shot several people in the yard. They took us once on a walk around. So on the walk, there was blood-- dried blood. I don't know what was going on. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Then, the-- on the 18th, or on 17th probably of-- or 17th of September, they took out a group of people, and took over at night. I didn't know what was going on because they were not shot. And I didn't know whom they took out. Of course, I had some other very unpleasant encounters with-- in the prisoner, I was beaten up. Since then, I have tinnitus in my ear-- in this-- and I lost--

speaker: interviewer
text: And to-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] on the 17th-- it seems so, yes-- of September, to my big surprise, I received a package. From whom? And when I started to know-- so I said, maybe there is something that-- and the bread was cut in pieces.

speaker: interviewee
text: But in one of the pieces, I felt a paper. There is a thin cigarette paper. I said-- and this is Kzarnowski, the supervisor from the [NON-ENGLISH]. I said, Meljinski, Jews from Lida ghetto transported the 18th. Tell that you are a Jew. You know what to do on the transport in Polish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Next day, I was taken for interrogation. Are you Jewish? Look, I said, sir, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you beat me, and anyways, otherwise you'll shot me, kill me. You want to tell that I am Jewish. I tell that I am Jewish.

speaker: interviewee
text: I can tell you even if you want to-- I'll tell you anything you want. Ha ha, sign it. So I read, you know, a stupid paper that I was arrested on suspicion that I am a Polish partisans but turn out that I am Jewish who from free will admitted that he is Jewish-- nothing that-- I signed it-- I send it back. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] At that time, I had to bury during the so-called dinner time, when [NON-ENGLISH] who brought in the big soup, you know. He hit me [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] with the-- with a stick. And he try to hit my head. But he hit me here. The stick-- that stick, you know, it's a two-by-four, or something. Broke in two. But then I decided, I am going to die anyway. I jumped to him and I beat him up there. And they put me in the cell and didn't touch me for two days. It was probably on 21st or so-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] [INAUDIBLE], pick up your things, and so on. And I was taken out from the prison. They put on the truck.

speaker: interviewer
text: And taken-- I said, where am I being taken? To a regular station. Then one of the policeman who beat me before, said, look, give me your boots. I'll give you something else.

speaker: interviewee
text: There where you're going, you don't need boots. I said, if there where I'm going I don't need boots, they'll take away there, I said. But the conversation was in a rough Polish language. I saw that it's probably the end of my-- so at this, I could use my tongue. I came up. And I saw a big, maybe 40, maybe 35, wagons, you know, with a lot of Jews around. And everybody tried to get you in one of those. The policeman took me by hand, and, you'll be in the last one. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So I said, look, probably here is my wife. I want to find my wife.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I ran from one to another, one to another, to wagon, Sula Staropolska. Finally, somebody says to me, there's Staropolska who, with all family, was taken by the first-- by the first transport on 18th. This what I heard that commotion in there.

speaker: interviewee
text: All right, and policeman all the time near me, step by step, step by step. And then he threw me into-- so I came in there. I stood there. There were some Jews I never know who were. So they looked terrible, as maybe somebody has shaved. Or so somebody gave me a-- I shaved myself, more or less brought myself to the water, you know. I didn't wash for so many days, now I've got-- and at night, toward the evening, the train started to go. And about midnight, it stopped at Moeste. It is a crossing station over the river Neman. And everybody, get out, get out, get out. And so I knew that place. And he didn't cross the river. But I know that here is the forest.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I said, aha, this is the end. They are going to shoot us in the forest. So I decided-- so I started to go toward the forest. I started to go faster, policeman after me. So I turn around, what did it matter? I want to do something. You have to stay and look at-- you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: All right, so he said stood there about 10 steps behind me. And I saw that I cannot get rid of it. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So then I turned around. And he went out. So I-- and the train started to move. I was only one left. So and train started to move. So I jumped in into the third wagon. And it was near the passenger wagon. And I saw in the passenger wagon there are officers and some Jews who were very well dressed and so on. And they were in conversation. I didn't know in that. I came. And there I met some people who I knew, by the name Ilutowicz-- girl. Make it short. They locked the wagons. We went-- went to a place called-- a town called [NON-ENGLISH]. From [NON-ENGLISH], I-- Ilutowicz said, the rich people got-- she knew me and all, because her sister or her cousin was a student, was married to one of the doctors, you know. So we started to talk, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I said, look, we have to somehow to get out because they are going-- who we are going to be all shot somewhere. There was a small kid. He said, I'll go with you. I said, first before going, we have to open the door. So the window over the door was with barbed wire. So I used my, you know, silver. And the train went-- during the train move-- so I opened the wire, looked out-- how the door was locked. And it was locked only a-- on the what you call it in English? With one bolt, so if you move the bolt, the doors open. But how to reach there? We had nothing.

speaker: interviewee
text: In [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [NON-ENGLISH], they threw us from one line-- lane to another lane, maneuvering the train. And the one with us-- I saw the railroad man. So I told him. We are taken. But I cannot tell him. So I thought, look, if you don't want to tell a Jew. So tell that Polish officer. He looked at me and said, [NON-ENGLISH]-- mister senior, you're going to look-- you're going to Mydonic. So he said, look, on the road here-- and I knew that area quite well for different reasons because I did research on my dissertation there. When you will be uphill, go very slow, as if you-- the locomotive cannot pull. But the moment you hit the other side of the hill, run all power.

speaker: interviewer
text: We said, the doors are locked. I said, leave it to me. So what really happened? With this small child, I opened the wire.

speaker: interviewee
text: I pulled out. I held him by the leg, hoisting him back and forth. And we opened the door. I pull him in.
